stories.headline: IMF chief warns Iran conflict will cause higher prices and slower growth globally
stories.summary: The IMF chief issued a stark warning that Iran's war is impacting the global economy, predicting that all indicators point toward higher prices and slower economic growth. The statement highlights how the conflict is creating ripple effects across international markets and monetary conditions.
stories.original_title: 'All roads lead to higher prices and slower growth,' warns IMF chief as Iran war hits global economy
stories.context: The International Monetary Fund, or IMF, is basically like the world's financial doctor—an organization that watches over the global economy and advises countries on how to keep their money systems healthy. When countries fight or go to war, it doesn't just affect them—it sends shockwaves through the entire world's money system. Wars make people nervous about the future, which causes them to buy less stuff and companies to produce fewer goods. This makes prices go up (what economists call inflation) because there's less stuff available while everyone still needs the same amount. At the same time, economic growth slows down because businesses and countries spend money on military actions instead of making products or services that help the economy grow.
stories.why_it_matters: When countries fight, it doesn't just hurt them - it hurts everyone's wallet. The war with Iran will make everyday stuff like food and gas more expensive and slow down how fast the world economy grows. This means regular people might pay more for everything while businesses struggle as much.
stories.other_side: The IMF may be exaggerating the impact to get attention or push their own agenda. Some experts believe the global economy is strong enough to handle the shock and won't be as badly affected as predicted.

stories.headline: Aluminium supply squeeze creates trading opportunity amid Iran tensions
stories.summary: Aluminium prices have surged due to global supply disruptions triggered by Middle East tensions and Iran's missile strikes on smelters. Religare Broking expects the rally to continue as structural deficits persist, with MCX Aluminium trading above key support levels.
stories.original_title: Commodity Radar: Aluminium supply squeeze offers opportunity as Religare suggests targets — Aluminium prices surged amid global supply disruptions triggered by Middle East tensions, including Iran's missile strikes on smelters. Religare Broking expects the rally to continue as structural deficits persist. MCX Aluminium trades above key support level…
stories.context: Aluminium is a lightweight metal that's used everywhere—from soda cans and airplane parts to construction materials and cars—making it crucial to modern industry. A "supply squeeze" means there's suddenly less aluminium available to buy, which naturally makes the price go up because lots of people still need it but there's not enough to go around. When there's conflict in the Middle East, it often disrupts shipping routes and production facilities, meaning factories can't make as much metal or ships can't safely transport it. This creates what economists call a "structural deficit"—a fancy way of saying that even when temporary problems end, there will still be more aluminium needed than can be produced for a long time, keeping prices high.
stories.why_it_matters: Aluminum is used to make everything from soda cans to cars and airplanes. When the price goes up because of supply problems from the Middle East conflict, those higher costs eventually trickle down to regular consumers buying products. This could mean more expensive cars, construction materials, and everyday items that use aluminum.
stories.other_side: The current price jump might be temporary panic buying that will settle down once the situation becomes clearer. Some experts warn that betting on continued price increases could backfire if supply chains adjust faster than expected.

stories.headline: Cantor Fitzgerald reiterates overweight rating on Applied Materials
stories.summary: Cantor Fitzgerald has reaffirmed its overweight rating for Applied Materials (AMAT), identifying the semiconductor equipment maker as one of the top 10 AI chip stocks to buy according to hedge funds. The rating underscores confidence in Applied Materials' position in the AI infrastructure market.
stories.original_title: Cantor Fitzgerald Reaffirms Overweight Rating for Applied Materials (AMAT)
stories.context: Applied Materials is a company that builds the enormous, complex machines that other companies use to manufacture computer chips—think of them as selling the factory equipment that makes the brains inside your phone, laptop, and self-driving car. "AI chips" are specialized processors designed specifically for artificial intelligence tasks like understanding human language, recognizing images, or driving autonomous vehicles, and they're becoming incredibly valuable as AI technology becomes more important in our daily lives. When investment firms like Cantor Fitzgerald give an "overweight rating," they're essentially saying this particular company is better than most others in the same field and investors should own more of it than the average stock. Hedge funds are investment companies that manage money for wealthy people and institutions, and when many of them agree on which stocks to buy, it often signals they see something promising happening in that industry.
stories.why_it_matters: Applied Materials makes the machines that build the computer chips needed for AI technology. If a major investment firm is recommending this stock, it suggests they believe AI will keep growing and need more chips, which could create job opportunities and new products. This shows where smart investors think the next big technology boom will come from.
stories.other_side: Investment firms often get these predictions wrong, and the AI boom might not last as long or be as profitable as they expect. The company's stock could drop if AI spending slows down or competitors offer better technology at lower prices.

stories.headline: Newmark Group arranges $525 million refinancing for The Artise property
stories.summary: Newmark Group (NMRK) announced it has arranged a $525 million refinancing package for The Artise, demonstrating the company's capabilities in commercial real estate finance. Newmark is recognized as one of the top PropTech stocks by hedge funds for its technology-driven real estate services.
stories.original_title: Newmark Group (NMRK) Arranges $525 Million Refinancing of The Artise
stories.context: Commercial real estate means buildings and properties used for business purposes—office towers, shopping malls, apartment complexes, hotels, and warehouses—rather than houses for families. Refinancing is like getting a new loan to pay off an old one, typically done when interest rates change or when the original loan is about to end. It's similar to refinancing a house mortgage: you replace your existing loan with a new one that has different terms, hopefully better ones that save you money. Companies like Newmark Group are middle specialists who help connect commercial property owners with banks and investors willing to lend them money, earning fees for arranging these complicated financial deals. "PropTech" is a buzzword for property technology—using software and digital tools to make buying, selling, renting, and managing real estate more efficient.
stories.why_it_matters: When companies can borrow half a billion dollars to refinance property, it shows banks are still willing to lend money even when the economy is uncertain. This keeps people employed in construction and real estate, and helps prevent buildings from going empty or into foreclosure. It's a sign that the commercial property market, which includes office buildings and shopping centers, is still functioning.
stories.other_side: Taking on this much debt could be risky if property values drop or if the building can't generate enough money to pay back the loan. Some experts worry that easy lending like this could lead to problems down the road if the real estate market weakens.

stories.headline: Polymarket upgrades exchange stack with new USDC-backed collateral token
stories.summary: Prediction market platform Polymarket is upgrading its exchange infrastructure by replacing USDC.e with a new in-house USDC-backed collateral token. The move represents a significant technological and financial infrastructure change for one of the largest prediction markets in operation.
stories.original_title: Polymarket Swaps USDC.e for In-House Collateral Token
stories.context: A prediction market is essentially a betting platform where people buy and sell "contracts" that pay out money if real-world events happen—like who will win an election, whether it will rain tomorrow, or if a new product will launch on time. These platforms are like regular stock markets but instead of trading pieces of companies, you're trading predictions about future news. USDC is a type of "stablecoin," which is cryptocurrency designed to stay worth exactly one dollar, making it less volatile than Bitcoin or Ethereum that swing wildly in value. Stablecoins are backed by real money held in bank accounts, designed to combine the benefits of digital currency with the stability of traditional money. When Polymarket upgrades its "exchange stack," they're essentially replacing the underlying technology that runs their entire platform—the computer programs and systems that process trades and hold people's money.
stories.why_it_matters: Prediction markets are like betting on future events - people put money on what they think will happen. When this platform changes how it handles money, it affects thousands of people who use it to bet on things like elections or sports. This upgrade could make the platform safer to use or could create new problems for people trying to withdraw their winnings.
stories.other_side: Changing the technology behind how money moves could confuse users or create security risks that weren't there before. Some people might lose trust if they don't understand the new system or if something goes wrong during the switch.

stories.headline: Prediction market winnings create tax filing confusion for American traders
stories.summary: Americans who flocked to prediction markets last year now face uncertainty about how to properly report and pay taxes on their winnings. The lack of clear guidance has created confusion across the growing prediction market trading community as tax season approaches.
stories.original_title: Nobody Knows How to File Taxes on Prediction Market Wins
stories.context: Tax filing is the legal requirement to tell the government how much money you earned and pay your share to fund public services like roads, schools, and hospitals. The tax system has specific rules for different types of income—wages from jobs, profits from selling investments, and yes, winnings from gambling or betting—but prediction markets are so new that tax authorities haven't clearly explained how to categorize this type of income yet. This creates uncertainty because unclear rules mean people might accidentally pay too little (and face penalties later) or too much (losing money they could have kept). The problem is that prediction market winnings might legally count as gambling, investment income, or something else entirely—each category having different tax rates and reporting requirements—and without clear guidance, honest taxpayers are left guessing how to stay legal.
stories.why_it_matters: When people win money on prediction markets and don't know how to report it to the government, they could face fines or legal trouble later. This confusion might scare people away from using these platforms, which could slow down a growing type of online betting and trading. The government needs to figure out clear rules so everyone knows what to do.
stories.other_side: People should have known that any money they make from betting or trading could be taxed, just like regular income. The lack of clear rules is partly because these platforms are so new that the government hasn't caught up yet.

stories.headline: Virbac declares updated share count and voting rights for March 2026
stories.summary: French pharmaceutical company Virbac released an official declaration regarding its total number of voting rights and shares representing share capital as of March 2026. The regulatory filing provides shareholders with updated information on corporate governance and ownership structure.
stories.original_title: Virbac : Declaration of the number of shares and voting rights 03/2026
stories.context: When people buy shares (also called stock) in a company, they become partial owners and typically get voting rights that let them influence important company decisions, like who sits on the board of directors or major policy changes. However, not all shareholders have equal voting power—some special shares might give extra votes to early investors or company founders, and different classes of shares can have different voting privileges. Legal regulations in many countries require companies to regularly report exactly how many shares exist, how many voting rights are out there, and who owns what—this ensures transparency so investors know what they're buying and so the public can see who actually controls these major corporations. The declaration for March 2026 tells everyone how the company's ownership and control structure is set up at that particular time—imagine it like taking a snapshot of who owns pieces of the company and how much say each owner gets in important decisions.
stories.why_it_matters: When a French drug company updates how many votes each share gets, it changes who has power to make decisions about the company. This matters to anyone who owns stock in Virbac because it affects how much say they have in business decisions. It's like the company is updating the rulebook for how owners exercise their rights.
stories.other_side: This is just routine paperwork that companies do regularly and doesn't really change anything important for most shareholders. The real power in companies usually stays with the biggest investors regardless of these technical voting right adjustments.

stories.headline: Lassila & Tikanoja publishes 2025 Annual Report
stories.summary: Lassila & Tikanoja Plc has released its Annual Report for fiscal year 2025, providing shareholders and investors with comprehensive financial results and business performance data. The stock exchange release offers detailed insights into the company's operations and strategic positioning.
stories.original_title: Lassila & Tikanoja's Annual Report 2025 has been published
stories.context: An annual report is a detailed document that publicly-traded companies release once a year to show everyone—investors, employees, regulators, and the public—exactly how the business performed over the past twelve months. These reports are like a very detailed report card for a company: they show how much money came in, how much went out, what the company owned, what they owed, and what management thinks will happen next year. Shareholders are people who own pieces of the company through purchasing stock, and they need this information to decide whether to keep their investment, sell it, or buy more. Stock exchanges—marketplaces where people buy and sell stocks—require these reports to be public information because allowing people to invest without full knowledge of a company's finances would be unfair and potentially fraudulent.
stories.why_it_matters: Annual reports are like report cards for companies - they show how much money they made, what they spent money on, and how the business is doing. This information helps people decide if they want to invest money in the company or work there. A good report could mean the company grows and hires more workers, while a bad one could mean job cuts.
stories.other_side: These reports are often written carefully to make the company look better than it really is. The important details might be buried in complicated language, so regular people might not understand the real problems the company is facing.

stories.headline: New trading tool flint-trading enables backtesting across 100+ exchanges
stories.summary: A new trading and backtesting tool called flint-trading has been added to PyPI, allowing traders to backtest and execute trades on Drift and Hyperliquid platforms. The tool provides data from over 100 centralized exchanges via CCXT integration and features a local-first, free data model.
stories.original_title: flint-trading added to PyPI — Backtest and trade on Drift and Hyperliquid. Data from 100+ CEXs via CCXT. Local-first, free data.
stories.context: Backtesting means using historical data to test how a trading strategy would have performed in the past—kind of like simulating what would happen if you could go back in time and make trades using your system, then seeing if you would have made or lost money. This helps traders test their ideas without risk: if your strategy would have failed in the past, it probably won't work well now either. Exchanges are platforms where people buy and sell things—cryptocurrency exchanges trade digital money like Bitcoin, stock exchanges trade company shares, and commodities exchanges trade raw materials like oil, gold, and wheat. CCXT is a free software library (a collection of pre-written computer code) that connects to hundreds of different exchanges, letting programmers build trading tools that can access markets all over the world from one place. When a new tool launches that claims to work with "100+ exchanges," it means it can pull price data and execute trades across all those different marketplaces automatically.
stories.why_it_matters: This new tool lets regular people test their trading ideas using real data from over 100 different exchanges before risking actual money. It's like being able to practice investing with play money using real-world information, which could help people avoid losing their savings on bad trades. This makes sophisticated trading tools available to everyday people instead of just Wall Street professionals.
stories.other_side: Having access to backtesting tools might make regular people overconfident and think they can beat the market when they really can't. Past performance doesn't guarantee future success, so these tests might give people a false sense of security.

stories.headline: Forbes calls for end to European policies targeting US tech firms
stories.summary: Steve Forbes argues that Europe's digital trade policies unfairly penalize American technology companies including Apple, Google, and Meta. The commentary advocates for a Section 301 investigation to address what he describes as discriminatory practices against US tech giants.
stories.original_title: STEVE FORBES: Europe's attacks on US tech firms must stop. We have just the way to do it
stories.context: Europe and the United States sometimes don't agree on rules about technology companies that operate across borders—specifically American tech giants like Facebook (Meta), Google, Apple, and Amazon that hundreds of millions of Europeans use every day. European regulators have created laws that they say protect European citizens' privacy and ensure fair competition, but American critics argue these rules are designed to disadvantage US companies and help European businesses instead. Section 301 is a part of US trade law that allows the American government to investigate whether other countries are treating US companies unfairly and potentially impose penalties or restrictions in response—it's essentially a legal tool for addressing what America sees as unfair trade practices. The broader tension here reflects an ongoing global struggle over which country gets to set the rules for the internet: should European regulators be able to control how American tech companies operate globally, or should American companies primarily follow US rules?
stories.why_it_matters: Steve Forbes is saying that Europe is making unfair rules that specifically hurt American tech companies like Apple and Google. If this keeps happening, those companies might make less money or stop selling products in Europe, which could mean fewer jobs and higher prices for European consumers. This could lead to trade fights between America and Europe that hurt businesses on both sides.
stories.other_side: European countries have the right to make rules that protect their citizens' privacy and ensure fair competition in their markets. These rules weren't made just to hurt American companies - they're trying to prevent big tech companies from having too much control and potentially abusing their power.
